REPLICABILITY.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters (summary says Hyperparameters reported) and precisely defines the evaluation metric (Pearson's r), and SVD-based methods are parameter-free which aids reproducibility. However, the preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, one dataset (Genomics) and NYT access may be unclear, and code is not mentioned, while TBuckets-Clustering requires a Max Dist parameter whose value is not given. These gaps mean the method is only partially reproducible from the provided description.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a new framing — measuring topic quality via thematic "buckets" of topic words and three concrete implementations (clustering, SVD, SVD-Reorg) which is a distinguishable idea compared to existing coherence measures. However, it primarily applies established tools (pretrained GloVe, agglomerative clustering, SVD) to the well-studied topic coherence problem, so the technical novelty is moderate rather than radical. The SVD-based, parameter-free variants and empirical improvement over Röder et al. on two of three datasets provide useful practical contribution, but overall this reads as an incremental, non-trivial extension rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized (introduction -> methods describing TBuckets-Clustering/SVD/SVD-Reorg -> experiments on 20NG, NYT, Genomics -> conclusion) and states the main contributions early (proposal of TBuckets, three creation techniques, and claimed improvements over Röder et al. 2015). Notation and scoring components (e.g., WL, EVS, singular-value weighting) are presented and hyperparameters are reported, suggesting consistency and clarity. Minor presentation gaps remain: the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables/examples are shown or fully effective, and the authors label writing quality only as adequate rather than polished, so a few small clarity improvements may be possible.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments compare TBuckets (three variants) against the state-of-the-art reported by Röder et al. (2015) on three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and show improved performance on two of three, which provides solid empirical support. However, sensitivity/ablation analysis appears limited (e.g., the clustering variant requires a Max Dist parameter but no detailed sensitivity study is reported) and the claimed utility for weakly supervised text classification is not clearly evidenced in the summary. Related work coverage is described as thorough and some limitations are discussed (parameter need for clustering, domain-specific word-similarity issues), and hyperparameters are reported, though code availability is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility. Overall the evaluation is strong but has minor gaps in ablations and broader validation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are supported by experiments on three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) using Pearson's r against human judgments, and it reports TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforming the Röder et al. (2015) baselines on 2/3 datasets. However, no mathematical derivations or theoretical justification for choices like weighting SVD scores by the square of the top singular value are described in the summary, and there is no mention of statistical significance testing or how overall performance was aggregated across datasets. The approach also retains a tunable parameter for the clustering variant, and the summary notes domain limitations (Genomics), so while results are promising, some methodological and theoretical gaps limit full technical confidence.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses topic coherence for LDA using embedding- and SVD-based methods (TBuckets) and evaluates against human judgments with Pearson's r on standard NLP datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics), which squarely situates it within the NLP/ML community. It compares to the canonical baseline (Röder et al., 2015), reports hyperparameters, and proposes parameter-free SVD variants, matching the methods and evaluation standards expected at top venues. The stated limitations (domain-specific similarity issues, a clustering hyperparameter) and future work are appropriate and do not undermine relevance to the conference audience.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of automatic topic coherence measurement with a novel and interpretable idea (grouping topic words into thematic "buckets") and introduces practical, parameter-free SVD-based variants. TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforms the state-of-the-art reported by Röder et al. (2015) on two of three datasets (NYT and 20NG) and the approach is reported to be useful for weakly supervised text classification, indicating practical utility. However, its uneven performance on Genomics and the acknowledged need for better domain-specific word similarities limit broad immediate adoption, so the contribution is useful and likely to be built upon but not yet transformative.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against the established state-of-the-art coherence methods reported by Röder et al. (2015) and evaluates on the same datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) using Pearson's r with human judgments, indicating fair conditions. The authors report when TBuckets loses (Genomics) and note domain-specific limitations, and they analyze method differences (clustering vs SVD, parameter-free SVD variants, bucket-based scoring components). Related work coverage is described as thorough, so comparisons are good though not evidently exhaustive of newer methods beyond Röder et al., hence a minor omission.

main_claims: Propose TBuckets, a novel approach that groups topic words into thematic 'buckets' to measure LDA topic quality, where a single large bucket indicates high coherence.
Introduce three bucket-creation techniques: TBuckets-Clustering, TBuckets-SVD and TBuckets-SVD-Reorg.

TBuckets (specifically TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) outperforms the state-of-the-art reported by Röder et al. (2015) on 2 out of 3 datasets (NYT and 20NG) and the TBuckets approach demonstrates better performance than state-of-the-art results overall.
The SVD-based TBuckets techniques are parameter-free, and TBuckets is useful for weakly supervised text classification.
_summary.methodology: Represent topic words using pre-trained 300-dimensional GloVe word embeddings and group them into buckets using (i) agglomerative clustering (TBuckets-Clustering), (ii) singular value decomposition on word vectors (TBuckets-SVD), and (iii) SVD with reorganization (TBuckets-SVD-Reorg); compute topic coherence from properties of the largest bucket (size, LDA order weight WL, and for SVD methods an eigenvector similarity EVS, with SVD scores weighted by the square of the highest singular value) and evaluate by Pearson's r correlation with human coherence judgments.
baselines: State-of-the-art methods as reported in Röder et al. (2015)
metrics: Pearson's r (correlation between automatic coherence scores and human judgments)
limitations: TBuckets-Clustering requires one parameter (Max Dist) for agglomerative clustering.
Authors note the need for better ways to compute word similarities suitable for specific domains like Genomics (stated as future work).
Authors plan to devise a unified framework to evaluate and employ various scoring metrics (stated as future work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - topic modeling / topic coherence measurement
